message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>After initial COVID-19 disease, immune dysregulation may persist and drive post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC). We described longitudinal trajectories of cytokines in adults up to 6 months following SARS-CoV-2 infection and explored early predictors of PASC.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>RECoVERED is a prospective cohort of individuals with laboratory-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection between May 2020 and June 2021 in Amsterdam, the Netherlands. Serum was collected at weeks 4, 12 and 24 of follow-up. Monthly symptom questionnaires were completed from month 2 after illness onset onwards; lung diffusion capacity (DLCO) was tested at 6 months. Cytokine concentrations were analysed by human magnetic Luminex screening assay. We used a linear mixed-effects model to study log-concentrations of cytokines over time, assessing their association with socio-demographic and clinical characteristics that were included in the model as fixed effects.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    186/349 (53%) participants had ≥2 serum samples and were included. Of these, 101 (54%: 45/101[45%] female, median age 55 years [IQR=45-64]) reported PASC at 12 and 24 weeks after illness onset. We included 37 reference samples (17/37[46%] female, median age 49 years [IQR=40-56]). PASC was associated with raised CRP and abnormal diffusion capacity with raised IL10, IL17, IL6, IP10 and TNFα at 24 weeks in the multivariate model. Early (0-4 week) IL-1
                    <jats:bold>β</jats:bold>
                    and BMI at illness onset were predictive of PASC at 24 weeks.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Our findings indicate that immune dysregulation plays an important role in PASC pathogenesis, especially among those individuals with reduced pulmonary function. Early IL-1
                    <jats:bold>β</jats:bold>
                    shows promise as predictors of PASC.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Inflammatory profiles are associated with long COVID up to 6 months after illness onset: a prospective cohort study of individuals with mild to critical COVID-19
